quotes.text: In regard to the confession of lesser sins, Brigham Young has counseled: If you have committed a sin that no other person on the earth knows of, and which harms no other one, you have done a wrong and sinned against your God, but keep that within your own bosom, and seek to God and confess there, and get pardon for your sin. If children have sinned against their parents, or husbands against their wives, or wives against their husbands, let them confess their faults one to another and forgive each other, and there let the confession stop; and then let them ask pardon from their God. Confess your sins to whoever you have sinned against, and let it stop there. If you have committed a sin against the community, confess to them. If you have sinned in your family, confess there. Confess your sins, iniquities, and follies, where that confession belongs. (JD 4:79.)
quotes.speaker: Daniel H. Ludlow
quotes.source: A Companion to Your Study of the Old Testament
quotes.attr: Daniel H. Ludlow, A Companion to Your Study of the Old Testament, 164

notes: The word translated "trespass" in the King James Version usually comes from a Hebrew word meaning "guilt" or another word meaning "unfaithful" or "treacherous." In contrast, the English word sin is usually the translation of a Hebrew word meaning "miss the mark or way," "go wrong." (Daniel H. Ludlow, A

quotes.text: Scriptures teach us more about the word atonement. The Old Testament has many references to atonement, which called for animal sacrifice. Not any animal would do. Special considerations included: o the selection of a firstling of the flock, without blemish, 22 [See Lev. 5:18; Lev. 27:26] o the sacrifice of the animal's life by the shedding of its blood, 23 [See Lev. 9:18] o death of the animal without breaking a bone, and 24 [See Ex. 12:46; Num. 9:12] o one animal could be sacrificed as a vicarious act for another. 25 [See Lev. 16:10]
quotes.speaker: Russell M. Nelson
quotes.attr: Russell M. Nelson, "The Atonement," Ensign, Nov 1996, 33
